speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Mark Bernstein. And this is slate number 2. What were the first signs that you experienced that things were changing towards the Jews? What was the first time you realized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first sign was is that you, you couldn't go out at night without a weapon on you, something to protect yourself. Well, you were called dirty Jews. And if you had a store, your window was broken. Even the, the first sign was-- is where they attacked you, is when you come out of shul. That, that was the very first sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was happening now in an organized way--

speaker: interviewee
text: An organized way.

speaker: interviewer
text: --under the fascists?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized way-- the Hungarian soldiers or the-- or the SS that, that came into the-- Hungary. That-- they were-- they made a habit out of it every Friday night. Or sometimes, they got the beating of their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because, like, my brother, who is very athletic-- he went to the-- it's-- I don't know what you call a gymnasium? A college? And he had a teacher who was an Olympic wrestler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one incident where they attacked us. And my brother went into the barbershop, pulled the head piece off the barber's chair and broke some heads. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, when, when they got beaten, they ran off. But when they succeeded, they stayed until people went home on our bloody heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about what time was this that this sort of thing is happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is usually at night, at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what year-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1943, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any specific anti-Jewish measures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no, nothing particular. They just wanted to hurt you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the changes that took place in your town as time went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: As-- after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Change-- the, the changes that took place is the-- that they took the Jews out. There was-- there was poverty after that. You know, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your town, didn't your-- what happened in your town itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: After they took us out, I don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but-- I mean while you're still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we're still there, it's-- we lived in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in-- you managed to stay living in your home, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your town become a ghetto at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, become a ghetto when the, the Germans came in. And they made an announcement. They sent out leaflets that all the Jews will have to move into a ghetto. They fenced off a large area of a mostly Jewish community. If any Gentile lived there, they had to, you know, move out. I happened to-- have to-- where we lived, the ghetto became across the street from us. So we had to go across.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you went-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wound up in the house where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you had moved from that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to leave-- take whatever the-- whatever room we got. We got the room when I was born in, in, in the ghetto. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, they made the best of it with livestock and-- all right, we didn't have any livestock. But where, where we moved into the ghetto, there was a wholesale wine dealer that had livestock. And there was a-- he had a cow. And we got a little milk out of it, you know, for the-- for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the full area of the size of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The full area-- I would say about six blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a part of Debrecen.

speaker: interviewee
text: A part of Debrecen. Said It's almost the-- in the-- in the heart of Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this change take place? In what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the spring of 1944, I don't remember exactly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did the ghetto have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no particular name. It said, Debrecen, you know, the ghetto, because they bought in from the small villages, some people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans and the Hungarians, and jointly-- military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any assigned duty in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- when the ghetto was closed, when they all-- all the Jews were put-- and the Voice of America warned the Hungarians that the day you close the ghetto, we're going to bomb the city. And they lived up to their promise. And my-- the assignment was they took men, young fellows from the ghetto, after the bombing to pick up dead bodies and take them to the-- up to the forest where they were-- cremation. Because there were so many dead soldiers, German soldiers, that they couldn't bury them fast enough. And I, I did electrical work for the electric company where the wires were [INAUDIBLE]. They were-- it was out. That's-- in Debrecen, the wires are outdoors, you know, on the pole, like some places here. And I went out to do work for them. And I was able to get food to bring in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know how to do electrical work?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I know? I was very interested in it as a kid. It started with bells--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had already--

speaker: interviewee
text: --low-voltage bells. And I had the experience. And I, I also was an apprentice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the, the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you already knew about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about it, yeah. And it was very challenging. I used to climb up roofs and put antennas up for people. In Europe, you need an antenna for radio, you know, between chimneys. And I used to go up, climb on the slate roofs-- just a daring kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you were doing this work, then, for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a pass system that allowed you outside of the ghetto? Or how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't any-- you wore a, a armband with a Jewish star. And if the guy that took you out, the soldier, he was nice, he let you take it off. But most of the time, you wore it. But if you-- if he was nice, you know, he'd let you take it off. You could go into a store. If you had some money, you could buy something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were allowed to do that sometimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, occasionally, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It depends who you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends who the guard was.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what kind of living space did you and your family live? Did, did they share the house with other people? Or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many other people would you say were living in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the house, there was about five or six families. But we had two rooms for ourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share the kitchen, the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, kitchen and bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the conditions there and in the ghetto in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto in general, very crowded, very crowded, very fearful. Especially if you were-- if you come from a family with a little bit of wealth. And the Germans or the Hungarians would come into the ghetto every day. And they'll take people that, that had businesses and take them out in a van where you don't see them-- in an enclosed van-- and torture them to get their money that they're-- if they have any fortune hidden-- gold. And when they brought them back, they were either mentally unable to function or physically unable to function from the beating that they took. And that was also one of the reasons that my father left.

speaker: interviewer
text: He left before it became a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. My father left before the Germans came into Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't know at that point that there was going to be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't. At that time, no, he didn't. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother, you know, made him leave as soon as he could, you know. Because God knows what would have happened if he stayed there. Because he was a well-known person, a respected citizen. And he would have been probably-- maybe would have been tortured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get any information to your father once you were living in the ghetto from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He didn't know-- I mean, he heard whatever he heard on the radio in America in Brooklyn. But he didn't know where we were, whether we were in Auschwitz or whether we were alive, until we got back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you manage to eat in, in the ghetto? How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, there wasn't a great shortage of food. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had bread and meat, you know, because we, we stored up. We stocked up on things-- and flour. And you baked-- you did your own baking. As a matter of fact, they, they set up sort of a makeshift hospital. And that was in-- one of the houses was an apartment house. And being that I knew about electricity, they asked me to wire up the x-ray machine. I had never done it before, but I figured it out. And I did it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had people with nervous breakdowns, you know. They just cracked up, went out of their mind, locked up in a room-- that the Germans took out and did a job on them. They brought them back. They weren't able to think for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these people personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, I knew them as people from shul, you know. I don't know their names. But I knew them as-- I remember. When I've been back the first time after-- you know, since I'm in America-- to Debrecen, I'm standing in front of the hotel. I met a man that used to belong to the shul. I recognized him because he had a few fingers missing. And I remember a child, you know. And I stopped him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I guess it happened at work or something. And when I told him who I was, he knew-- you know, my father was very well-known in Debrecen, you know, in the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the Jewish leaders in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the biggest leader that I know was-- his name was Waldman. He was a wholesale wine dealer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah. Well, I, I believe his name was Waldman. He always sat in the first row in shul. And those were-- there were the dignitaries, you know, that sat up front. As a matter of fact, I was born in his house, in, you know, he was the landlord. And that's where we were in the ghetto, in his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he well-liked? Or what did people think of him as a-- as a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- as a child, I remember he was very cold person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened is when the-- there was an opportunity for people to go to Switzerland when this-- when the Germans came in. It all depended on how much money you had. Like this particular man, he bought himself out of Hungary from the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: While he was still in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, before he even got into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm a bit confused--

speaker: interviewee
text: He went-- he went to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm-- I asked you who was the leader in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the ghetto, there was no leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I thought you meant the-- in general, in Debrecen, you know. No, in the ghetto, there was no leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Nazis run everything themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: They run everything themselves. There was no leader. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some Jews that turned against Jews. Those things-- I've seen it happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because that, that happened while I was in-- working on the farm in, in, in Austria. There, we had a man, a, a doctor-- or was he a lawyer, I'm not sure-- that was brown-nosing to the Germans. And he had me beat up pretty bad because I stole a blanket. And I stole some bread. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then I met him in Budapest after. And I paid him back my debt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paid him back-- sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I paid him back that, you know, what I owed him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when he had me beat up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems like a lifetime. But it was a month or two, maybe, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, they took us to the brick yard, the brick factory. We were there for a while because the brick factory, they had the railroad siding. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then Monday, they put us in a freight car-- 80 people in a-- 80, 100 people, men, women, and children. And we were destined for Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fortunately that the Germans bombed the railroad tracks-- I mean, the-- either the Americans or the Russians. And we-- they couldn't take us to Auschwitz. So we were-- we stood in a freight car six days and nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your-- the rest of your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: --aside from your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my two sisters. My brother was taken out of the ghetto earlier, to a labor camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: --because he was older. He, he wound up in Mauthausen and wound up in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He fought in a revolution in Budapest against the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And there were-- there were families, you know, pretty well together. And the-- what we-- people were dying in the car. We had no water. Food ran out, no sanitary facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you feeling and thinking during those six days that you were-- that it was not moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: That you're going to die right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there no-- was there any lights?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a little window up on top where-- we had these boy-scout coolers with water. The railroad siding, we stood there. And two nuns in, in like the penguin uniform, you know, that the nuns wore in those days, were standing there. And we tied together some clothes to lower down to ask them for some water. And instead of giving us water, they took away the jug, these--

speaker: interviewer
text: They took away--

speaker: interviewee
text: The water jug, the little thing. They're nuns. These were Catholic nuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- our final trip was Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Vienna, they opened the doors. And the people there would-- in a freight car, whoever died, stayed there. And the, the ones that are able to get out, get out in the daylight and, of course, look for water. That was the, the main thing that you look for, is-- without water, you can't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- what percentage of the people in the freight car, did you-- would you say died during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the 80, I would say 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15 died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone you knew among those people? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were mixed. And once we arrived, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive? When would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did we arrive, you mean? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. We arrived late in the afternoon-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the dates I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is 1944 still?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, I guess, yeah, '44. '44. I know there was-- they picked out of us. I think the youngest child was my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think-- and they marched us with our-- whatever belongings we had through Vienna to the outskirts onto an estate, to a farm. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there, we were able to bathe, you know. We could wash up, clean up. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my first job was to get behind the plow, to plow the field. The plow was taller than I was. And I was a big kid. I was tall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had an Ukrainian, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fascist guard. And, of course, it was fortunate for me that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, where was this, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside of Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in-- what, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Strasshof, I think-- the name of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know how to spell that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you try to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strasshof-- it's a German name, Austrian name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then the-- there was also Russian prisoners already there. And the guard was a Ukrainian. It wasn't military-- a civilian, Ukrainian guard that lived there at a house on the-- on the farm. And we had to plow the field with cows, not with horses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to a cow, you've got to tell him, [GERMAN] or, or, [GERMAN], you know, left, left or right. And that's how the cow would obey. And they would take [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 teams of cows. And when we went down the end of the field, they-- a big piece was done. And of course, I didn't know how to talk to them. And until I got to know it, I got whipped a few times because I got out of line. The cows moved, you know, went the wrong way. And I messed up the, the plowing. But I learned that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who whipped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who whipped you? Who whipped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Ukrainian guard. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they called you the dirty Jew and-- besides a few chosen words.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the guards, officers, or commandants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in, like, an organized camp? Or how--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this was an estate. This was a, a huge farm that belonged to an Austrian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His-- he, he lived there. His family lived there. It was-- you know, the houses itself was the perimeter, the fence. And there was a huge gate-- and the stables. In other words, it was like a circle. And there were barracks, you know, where, where they put-- built bunks where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was with my mother and two sisters and some other people. We had bunk beds, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know, with straw, hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any other people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Debrecen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to know new people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I got to know the people that we were-- were there, that we worked with. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember their names anymore. But I don't even know how many they-- how many survived. Because when-- after, they-- when they took us from that farm, a lot of them died out at the front lines, like, like my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that? What do you mean, the front lines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they were killed. They were shot by the Germans. The front lines was between the Germans and the Russians, where they were fighting The Russians were--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans took them with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Germans-- they tried to take us, too. But we ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What food were you given when you were in this farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: What food we were given-- there was one kitchen, where they-- a lot of starch, a lot of potatoes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soups and potatoes, no meat--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: --some bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way you supplemented that, that We were able to? Or was it enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: I supplemented a lot with tomatoes. Because I used to sneak out during the night and go into the tomato field and steal tomatoes and bring it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that nearby, the tomato field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I also-- I made a, a basket where I used to steal apples from, from the, the local homes that had apple trees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a long stick-- I would reach in over the fence and just let, let the apple fall into a little basket. When you're hungry, there's reason-- means and ways of finding food, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother know you were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened to you if you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they would have caught me? I could have been, you know, beaten up or shot. I mean, I was so close to it so many times, that I-- I didn't worry. I had no fear. I had no fear. I've been so close to getting killed. I-- they had me watch the potato field on the farm when the-- 11 craters, 11 bombs fell where I was sitting. There were baked potatoes. I, I saw the earth rise from a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? You were-- what were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was watching the potatoes--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was for--

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans don't come and steal. That was on a farm by the-- they took the people in to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were guarding the field for who?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Germans, for the-- for the Austrians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then why were you making sure that the Germans wouldn't steal potatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- that because the people would come and steal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you mean people who weren't-- who weren't soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, who wasn't soldiers-- the, the Austrians themselves, they would come and steal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also short of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Everything went for military. And there was a-- I heard the sirens from Vienna that the planes are coming. And I can-- heard a sound of the bombs from a distance. Then as far as my eye could see, I saw the earth rise. And I started running. And by time I reached the road, there were 11 craters-- 11 holes was where I was sitting, where I was watching. And when I went back, we had baked potatoes from the shrapnels. And underground was a radar station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the potato field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underneath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underneath, yeah, because there was an airport not far from there, a military airport. I've seen American planes shot down, American pilots shot in the air before they reached the ground. And they were parachuted. And they were-- parachute, they were floating down. And the next thing you saw is the SS with the motorcycles coming into the fields. And before they reached the ground, they killed them. They shot him. They stripped him of his stuff. That's how good these people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so we're just about to change tapes again.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
